source.title: ACIDES: on-line monitoring of forward genetic screens for protein engineering

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Forward genetic screens (DE, DMS) produce noisy NGS count data, making it hard to estimate variant performance (selectivity) and rank variants with confidence. Existing methods (e.g., Enrich2) use Poisson-based linear fitting, which poorly handles over-dispersion and non-linear enrichment. There is a need for on-line monitoring to decide when to stop experiments, saving resources.

section_gists.heading: ACIDES: on-line monitoring of forward genetic screens for protein engineering
section_gists.gist: Introduces ACIDES, a method to estimate variant selectivities with confidence intervals from noisy NGS data in DE/DMS. Addresses three problems: robust scoring, accurate error estimation, and convergence detection to guide experimental decisions. Applicable to various screening experiments.
process_signals: Problem: noisy NGS counts impede variant performance estimation.
Need: on-line monitoring to decide when to stop experiments.
Goal: extract maximal information, provide accurate statistical scores.
methods: Statistical inference combined with in-silico simulations.
Estimates selectivity and 95% confidence intervals.
Quantifies convergence via Rank Robustness (RR).

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Describes ACIDES workflow: estimate selectivity and confidence intervals, correct naive ranks, compute RR to assess convergence. Validates on synthetic data, showing improved ranking over naive methods.
process_signals: First step: estimate selectivity and 95% CI.
Second step: correct naive ranks using simulated scores.
RR quantifies convergence; high RR means robust ranking.
Synthetic data validation: ACIDES corrects winner's curse.
methods: Selectivity estimation via maximum likelihood.
Rank correction by simulating scores from normal distribution.
RR computed as overlap of top-50 variants between naive and simulated ranks.

section_gists.heading: Analysis of screening experiments with multiple time points
section_gists.gist: Applies ACIDES to 7 real datasets (phage-display, in-vivo DE, yeast assays). Computes corrected ranks and RR. Introduces Accuracy and Spread metrics to explain RR variations. Low RR indicates noise dominates; suggests further tests or more rounds.
process_signals: Datasets vary in RR (0.5 to >0.8).
Accuracy (inverse of average error) and Spread (score difference) explain RR.
Low RR in Data-E,G due to low Accuracy; Data-F due to low Spread.
When RR low, cannot distinguish fitness from noise.
methods: Accuracy: inverse of average predicted errors.
Spread: difference between top and 1000th variant scores.
failures_or_limits: Some datasets show low RR (~0.5), indicating unreliable ranking.
Low Accuracy or low Spread limit RR.

section_gists.heading: Integration into the experimental pipeline
section_gists.gist: Shows how ACIDES can guide experimental decisions by monitoring RR vs. rounds and NGS depth. Downsampling reveals that many experiments could have been stopped earlier. Saturation of RR indicates convergence.
process_signals: RR can be estimated after each round or with downsampled NGS.
Slow increase or saturation of RR suggests experiment can be ended.
Data-A: RR saturates at 5 rounds, 20% NGS depth; actual depth excessive.
Data-F: more rounds/depth would improve RR; Data-B: at saturation.
methods: Downsampling NGS data by factor ε using model resampling.
RR computed for different rounds and NGS depths.
metrics: RR as function of rounds and NGS depth
failures_or_limits: Different datasets show different saturation behaviors.
Assumes model correctly captures noise for downsampling.

section_gists.heading: Comparison with previous work
section_gists.gist: Compares ACIDES with Enrich2 using replicate data. ACIDES shows higher correlation of low-error scores between replicates, indicating better error estimation. Enrich2 fails with low counts. ACIDES outperforms especially for bulk variants.
process_signals: Enrich2 uses weighted linear fitting with Poisson variance.
ACIDES uses negative binomial likelihood, softmax normalization, and max likelihood.
ACIDES better captures non-linear enrichment and over-dispersion.
Enrich2 struggles with low-count variants.
methods: Comparison via R² of scores with low predicted errors between replicates.
Systematic selection of variants based on predicted error.
failures_or_limits: ACIDES improvement most visible for intermediate-score variants.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Summarizes ACIDES advantages: negative binomial noise model, softmax normalization, max likelihood inference. Outperforms Enrich2 and DiMSum in error estimation. Limitations: assumes model correctness, may not capture all noise sources, requires sufficient NGS depth. Future: extend to other experimental designs.
process_signals: Three-fold improvement over Enrich2: max likelihood, softmax, negative binomial.
Negative binomial better captures over-dispersion than Poisson (Fig. S1).
DiMSum tailored for two time-points with replicates; ACIDES more general.
Limitations: model assumptions, need for sufficient NGS depth.
methods: Negative binomial distribution with power-law dispersion.
Softmax normalization to handle dominant variants.
Maximum likelihood estimation.
failures_or_limits: Assumes model correctness; may not capture all noise sources.
Requires sufficient NGS depth for reliable inference.

section_gists.heading: Model
section_gists.gist: Defines exponential growth model with softmax normalization. Counts modeled by negative binomial with power-law dispersion (r=βλ^α). Likelihood maximization infers α, β, initial frequencies ρ₀ⁱ, and growth rates aⁱ. 95% CIs from curvature. Replicates share parameters.
process_signals: Hypothesis: negative binomial better than Poisson for over-dispersion.
Dispersion parameter r follows power-law of expected count λ.
Softmax normalization C_t inferred from data.
Model parameters shared across replicates.
methods: Exponential model: ρ_{t+Δt}ⁱ = C_t ρ_tⁱ exp(aⁱ Δt).
Negative binomial likelihood: NB(n_tⁱ | ρ_tⁱ N_t, β(ρ_tⁱ N_t)^α).
95% CIs from log-likelihood curvature.

section_gists.heading: Synthetic data
section_gists.gist: Generates synthetic counts from the model with given parameters. Used for validation in Fig. 1. Parameters chosen to simulate data-rich and data-poor scenarios.
process_signals: Synthetic data generated from the same model, assuming model correctness.
Parameters: α=0.69, β=0.8, aⁱ~N(-1,0.25²), log ρ₀ⁱ~N(1,1²).
Data-rich: M=5e4, N_t=1e7; data-poor: M=1e6, N_t=1e6.
methods: Sampling from negative binomial with specified parameters.
failures_or_limits: Assumes model is correct; may not reflect real noise.

section_gists.heading: Model inference
section_gists.gist: Two-step iterative algorithm: (1) infer ρ₀ⁱ, aⁱ with fixed α,β; (2) infer α,β with bias correction via teacher-student simulation. Uses subset of variants for efficiency. Gauge choice resolves redundancy.
process_signals: Direct gradient inference of α,β produces bias; corrected by simulation.
Subset selection: variants with >0 counts in >2 rounds and total count > threshold.
Threshold 100 for most datasets, 10000 for Data-E,-G due to higher noise.
Iterative between (ρ₀ⁱ,aⁱ) and C_t.
methods: Two-step iterative gradient descent.
Teacher-student framework for bias correction.
Subset selection to reduce computation.
failures_or_limits: Bias in α,β inference without correction.
Subset threshold may need tuning per dataset.

section_gists.heading: Simulated rank and rank robustness (RR)
section_gists.gist: Defines naive rank (by aⁱ) and corrected rank (median and 95% CI from 3000 simulations using δaⁱ). RR is overlap of top-50 between naive and simulated ranks, averaged over simulations.
process_signals: Corrected rank accounts for estimation uncertainty.
RR measures ranking stability; high RR means robust top variants.
Uses top 5000 variants after discarding high-error ones.
methods: Simulate scores from N(aⁱ, (δaⁱ)²) 3000 times.
Compute median and 95% CI of ranks.
RR = average overlap of top-50 / 50.

section_gists.heading: NGS-Downsampling for Fig. 3
section_gists.gist: Downsamples NGS data by factor ε using model resampling with reduced total counts. Re-estimates aⁱ with known α,β, then computes RR. Rescaling removes minor bias. Validated against actual read-reduced data (Fig. S5).
process_signals: Downsampling simulates lower sequencing depth.
Uses estimated parameters to generate synthetic data with εN_t.
Rescaling to focus on trend, not absolute RR.
Validated on synthetic data.
methods: Resample counts from NB with εN_t.
Re-estimate aⁱ using known α,β.
Compute RR and rescale by RR at ε=1.
failures_or_limits: Assumes model is correct for downsampling.
Minor bias from resampling corrected by rescaling.

section_gists.heading: Cross validation test for Fig. 5
section_gists.gist: Leave-one-out cross-validation for two time-point data. Splits replicates into train/test. Infers α,β from train, then estimates enrichment statistics for both. Computes z-score between train and test enrichments. Evaluates z-score distribution against standard normal using inverse std dev and Q-Q plot R².
process_signals: Tests accuracy of predicted errors.
ACIDES uses resampling from negative binomial; others use Poisson or empirical variance.
z-score should be standard normal if errors are accurate.
Metrics: 1 - |1 - std(z)| and R² of Q-Q plot.
methods: Leave-one-out cross-validation.
Enrichment fⁱ = log((n₁ⁱ/n₀ⁱ) * (n_wt⁰/n_wt¹)).
zⁱ = (f̄_trainⁱ - f̄_testⁱ) / sqrt(σ_trainⁱ² + σ_testⁱ²).
Evaluate z-score distribution.
failures_or_limits: Assumes two time-points; may not generalize to multiple rounds.
Other methods may have different error models.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Noisy NGS counts in forward genetic screens hinder accurate variant ranking and convergence detection.
A negative binomial model with power-law dispersion better captures over-dispersion than Poisson.
Maximum likelihood estimation with softmax normalization improves selectivity inference over linear fitting.
Rank Robustness (RR) can guide experimental decisions on when to stop screening.
ACIDES outperforms Enrich2 and DiMSum in error estimation, especially for low-count and intermediate variants.
uncertainties_to_resolve: How does ACIDES perform when the negative binomial assumption is violated?
Can the method be extended to more complex experimental designs (e.g., multiple time-points with replicates)?
What is the minimum NGS depth required for reliable RR estimation?
How sensitive is the inference to the choice of subset threshold?
Does the teacher-student bias correction fully remove bias in α,β estimation?

events.summary: NGS data from forward genetic screens are vast and noisy, impeding estimation of variant performance.
Scores should come with precise statistical error estimation to avoid winner's curse.
It is hard to know when to end the experiment: too few rounds may select weak variants, too many rounds are costly.
ACIDES combines statistical inference and in-silico simulations to improve performance estimation.
ACIDES estimates selectivity and 95% confidence interval for each variant using maximum likelihood estimation.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk introduces the problem, the ACIDES method design, and key components like selectivity estimation, confidence intervals, negative binomial noise model, and Rank Robustness.

events.summary: ACIDES uses maximum likelihood estimation with negative binomial noise model to estimate variant selectivities and confidence intervals.
Negative binomial model shows 50- to 70-fold improvement over Poisson in predicting NGS noise on plasmid library data.
ACIDES corrects naive rank bias using in-silico simulations and computes Rank Robustness (RR) to assess selection convergence.
ACIDES applied to synthetic data-rich and data-poor cases; high RR in data-rich, low RR in data-poor, with true ranks within 95%-CI.
Analysis of seven screening datasets reveals RR varies from ~0.5 to >0.8, explained by Accuracy and Spread metrics.
Low RR indicates inability to distinguish fitness from noise; suggests further tests or more experimental efforts.
ACIDES monitors RR vs. rounds and NGS depth; downsampling shows Data-A could have been stopped earlier, while Data-F needs more effort.
numbers_or_metrics: RR saturates at 5 rounds, 20% NGS depth

events.summary: ACIDES compared with Enrich2 using replicate data; ACIDES shows higher correlation of low-error scores between replicates.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers ACIDES design, synthetic validation, real data analysis, limitations, experimental integration, and comparison with Enrich2.

events.summary: Comparison of ACIDES with Enrich2 using replicate data to test accuracy of predicted errors.
ACIDES outperforms Enrich2 in replicate consistency, especially for variants with low predicted errors.
Systematic selection of variants by predicted error magnitude to test consistency across replicates.
ACIDES consistently outperforms Enrich2 across all 12 pairs of technical replicates in Data-A and Data-B.
ACIDES and DiMSum show similar performance in inverse standard deviation of z-scores, both outperforming Enrich2.
ACIDES slightly outperforms DiMSum in R^2 score from Q-Q plot, reflecting better higher-order statistics.
ACIDES demonstrates higher capacity to recover ground truth rank in synthetic data scenarios.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk focuses on experimental comparisons and validation of ACIDES against Enrich2 and DiMSum using replicate and synthetic data.

events.summary: ACIDES first step estimates variant selectivity via log-likelihood maximization with softmax normalization and negative binomial noise model, improving over linear fitting.
ACIDES outperforms Enrich2 and DiMSum in error estimation, especially for bulk variants with intermediate scores, as shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. 5.
ACIDES second step uses bootstrap to correct naive rank bias and compute 95% confidence intervals for variant ranks.
ACIDES third step introduces Rank Robustness (RR) to quantify convergence of selection experiment by measuring stability of top-50 variant ranks.
ACIDES does not account for changes in selection pressure over rounds, though constant in most analyzed datasets.
ACIDES assumes count variance depends only on variant frequency via negative binomial, may not capture sequence-dependent dispersion from error-prone PCR.
ACIDES cannot describe statistical errors from replicates not sharing the same initial library, as model is trained on a single series of screening rounds.
ACIDES cannot account for additional rounds of mutagenesis between selection rounds, potentially underestimating scores of newly generated variants.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk describes ACIDES design steps, validation against other methods, and limitations. It provides explicit process events with quotes.

events.summary: Subset selection criteria for model inference: variants with counts >0 in more than two rounds and total count above a threshold (100 for most datasets, 10000 for Data-E, -G due to higher noise).
Corrected rank estimation: generate synthetic scores from normal distribution with estimated mean and standard deviation, repeat 3000 times, compute median and 95% CI.
Rank Robustness (RR) computed as average overlap of top-50 variants between naive rank and synthetic naive ranks over 3000 simulations.
NGS downsampling: resample counts from likelihood with reduced total counts εN_t, re-estimate scores using known α,β, compute RR, rescale to remove bias.
Cross-validation test: leave-one-out, split replicates into train/test, infer parameters from train, compute enrichment statistics, evaluate z-score distribution against standard normal.
Input data format: count matrix with rows as variants and columns as experimental rounds, generated from FASTQ files after preprocessing (trimming, merging, QC).

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methodological steps for subset selection, rank correction, RR computation, downsampling, cross-validation, and data preprocessing, all central to the ACIDES pipeline.
This chunk contains only references, acknowledgements, author contributions, competing interests, and additional information. No research evolution events are described.

evidence_bank.quote: the vast and noisy nature of the sequencing data impedes the estimation of the performance of individual variants

in order to rank the variants and to identify the best performing ones, the score should come with a precise estimation of its statistical error. As a consequence of the noise in the counts, some irrelevant variants might appear to be highly enriched (winner's curse)

when running DE over multiple rounds, it is hard to know when to end the experiment: performing too few rounds could lead to selection of weak variants, not representative of their true ranking. On the other hand, performing too many rounds is costly, time-consuming and even ethically questionable

we propose ACIDES that combines statistical inference and in-silico simulations to improve performance estimation in the library selection process by attributing accurate statistical scores to individual variants
The first step of ACIDES estimates the selectivity of each individual variant present in the dataset (Fig. 1b) and its 95% confidence interval
our approach combines a robust inference framework (maximum likelihood estimation) with a better quantification of the NGS sampling noise
our approach benefits from a negative binomial distribution
we observed that our negative binomial model realizes a 50- to 70-fold improvement over the Poisson model in the predictive ability of the NGS sampling noise
The second step of ACIDES uses the estimation of the selectivities and their statistical errors to rank the variants.
We correct this bias using in-silico simulations
The third and last step of ACIDES uses simulations to quantify a Rank Robustness (RR), a measure of the quality of the selection convergence
we apply ACIDES to two synthetic datasets (Methods) describing two opposite scenarios
the estimated RRs are high and low for, respectively, the data-rich and -poor examples
In Data-A and Data-B, Accuracy is the highest, while the Spread is also relatively large. This is consistent with the high RR (RR > 0.8).
Data-F has a relatively high Accuracy, but Spread is the narrowest
we cannot distinguish if the obtained variants are selected because of their ability to perform the task (fitness) or just there due to noise
ACIDES can estimate RR after each selection round
ACIDES can be run on downsampled NGS data to compute RR with smaller NGS depth
At 1% NGS depth, the variant ordering seems largely unreliable: RR is smaller than 0.5
Saturation of RR seems to be observed for RR > 0.7, which corresponds to 5 performed rounds with the NGS depth larger than 20%
We start by comparing the performance of ACIDES with Enrich2
we plot the scores with low predicted errors obtained from one replicate against those obtained from the other
we compare ACIDES with a weighted linear least squares method (Enrich2)
The figure shows that ACIDES outperforms Enrich2.
ACIDES's correlation becomes more dominant as the set size decreases
For each set, we measure the correlation between two replicates
In all cases our approach outperforms the competitor (Fig. 4d).
we perform the comparison for all possible 12 pairs of technical replicates in Data-A and Data-B
ACIDES and DiMSum show similar performance and they both overperform Enrich2.
ACIDES slightly outperforms DiMSum and behaves better than the other algorithms.
demonstrate the higher capacity of ACIDES to recover the ground truth rank in several scenarios based on synthetic data
ACIDES' first step comes with a three-fold improvement over this last approach.
instead of relying on the linear least squares fitting, we estimate the score by log-likelihood maximization.
instead of a simple exponential growth of the counts, we included a softmax nonlinear function
ACIDES uses a negative binomial distribution to model the count variability
our approach realizes a more robust and accurate estimation of the variant scores and outperforms the previous method (Fig. 4).
ACIDES slightly outperforms DiMSum and remains better than the other previous methods.
The second step of ACIDES uses a bootstrap method to account for the bias and recover both the corrected rank and its 95%-CI.
as a third step of ACIDES, we introduce RR that describes how many of the top-50 variants in the naive rank are correct.
RR measures how stable and robust are the ranks of the variant selectivities.
ACIDES does not account for changes in the selection pressure over rounds.
ACIDES uses a negative binomial model to describe the dispersion of count data by assuming that the count variance depends only on the frequency of the variant.
it is possible that dispersions induced by a sequence-dependent procedure, such as error-prone PCR
statistical errors due to the replicates that do not share the same initial library cannot be described by ACIDES, provided that the model is only trained on a single series of screening rounds.
ACIDES can not account for additional rounds of mutagenesis performed between the selection rounds.
ACIDES will treat the newly generated variants as if they had zero counts at the beginning and the scores for these new variants may be slightly underestimated.

we use the variants that satisfy the following two criterions: (i) their counts are larger than 0 more than twice in the selection rounds and (ii) whose total NGS count (as summed over all the rounds) is above a threshold. We set this threshold to 100 for all the datasets except for Data-E -G, where 10000 is used.
Repeating this estimation 3000 times, we then compute the median and 95%-CI of the obtained synthetic naive ranks.

To estimate RR, we compare the top-50 variants in the naive rank and each synthetic naive rank. We count the number of overlaps between them and average it over the 3000 estimations. RR is computed by dividing the obtained overlap by 50.

To obtain downsampled count data $\tilde{n}_{t}^{i}$ ( $i=1,2,\ldots,M, t\in T$ ) by a factor $\epsilon$ , we sample synthetic data from the likelihood function (2) with a reduced number of the total counts $\epsilon N_{t}$
we rescale the downsampled RR using the RR obtained in a similar manner, but with $\epsilon=1$ . This is to remove a minor bias due to re-sampling
For Fig. 5, we perform a leave one out cross-validation test
The input for ACIDES is a count dataset matrix. Each row corresponds to an index (e.g., barcode) for each variant, while each column indicates the experimental round of DE or DMS.
This can be generated from the FASTQ file, following preprocessing depending on the experimental details.

In many applications with multiple time-points, such scores are computed as the variant enrichment that is defined as the logarithmic ratio between the variant frequencies in the last and second to last round $^{13}$ or between the last and first round $^{14,19,20,22,35,49}$ .
Other methods use instead unsupervised approaches to predict selectivities from the sequences of variants $^{55,69-72}$ .
Most of these methods rely on supervised algorithms, which are trained to predict the selectivity (output) from the sequence of a variant (input) $^{56,61-68}$ .

A more sophisticated approach that uses all the data consists in inferring the slope of a linear line fitted to the log-frequencies of variants over all the screening rounds/time points $^{24,25,36,40}$ .

For a given series of samples over screening rounds, we perform NGS and denote by $n_{t}^{i}$ the obtained count of the i-th variant ( $i=1,2,\ldots,M$ ) at round (time-point) $t\in T$ .
Since the 21 nucleotides are randomly and independently generated, we can use a position-weight matrix model to predict the frequency of each variant in the sample.

To quantify it, as a third step of ACIDES, we introduce RR that describes how many of the top-50 variants in the naive rank are correct.
In this comparison, we employ the cross-validation techniques used in $^{48}$ to calculate a z-scores of enrichment estimations for each variant (see Method for more details).
x% means the dataset where the number of NGS reads per round is reduced to x% (100% is the original dataset).
We start by comparing the performance of ACIDES with Enrich2, the state-of-the-art for estimating variant scores (selectivities) in multiple time-points experiments $^{41}$ .
At 1% NGS depth, the variant ordering seems largely unreliable: RR is smaller than 0.

To gain deeper insight into RR, we introduce the following two metrics: (i) Accuracy: this measures the accuracy of variant performance measurements, computed based on the inverse of average errors predicted by the model for the estimated scores (Table 1), and (ii) Spread: the extent to which the variants are intrinsically different in their performance for the task, calculated based on the difference between the estimated scores of the highest-scoring variant and the variant ranked 1000th (Table 1).

Specifically, RR is the ratio at which the top-50 variants in the naive rank are correctly identified (Methods).
Spread is the difference between the estimated scores of the highest and 1000th highest variants, normalized by the highest score.
Accuracy is the inverse of average errors predicted by the model for the estimated scores of the top 1000 variants, normalized by the highest score and divided by 10.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Noisy NGS count data in forward genetic screens hinders accurate estimation of variant performance and convergence detection, necessitating a method for robust scoring, error estimation, and on-line monitoring.

canonical_events.summary: NGS data from forward genetic screens are vast and noisy, impeding estimation of variant performance.
Scores need precise statistical error estimation to avoid winner's curse.
It is hard to know when to end the experiment: too few rounds may select weak variants, too many rounds are costly.
ACIDES combines statistical inference and in-silico simulations to improve performance estimation.
ACIDES estimates selectivity and 95% confidence interval for each variant using maximum likelihood estimation with negative binomial noise model and softmax normalization.
Negative binomial model shows 50- to 70-fold improvement over Poisson in predicting NGS noise on plasmid library data.
ACIDES corrects naive rank bias using in-silico simulations and computes Rank Robustness (RR) to assess selection convergence.
ACIDES applied to synthetic data-rich and data-poor cases; high RR in data-rich, low RR in data-poor, with true ranks within 95%-CI.
Analysis of seven screening datasets reveals RR varies from ~0.5 to >0.8, explained by Accuracy and Spread metrics.
Low RR indicates inability to distinguish fitness from noise; suggests further tests or more experimental efforts.
ACIDES monitors RR vs. rounds and NGS depth; downsampling shows Data-A could have been stopped earlier, while Data-F needs more effort.
key_numbers: RR saturates at 5 rounds, 20% NGS depth

canonical_events.summary: ACIDES compared with Enrich2 using replicate data; ACIDES shows higher correlation of low-error scores between replicates.
ACIDES outperforms Enrich2 and DiMSum in error estimation, especially for bulk variants with intermediate scores, as shown by cross-validation z-score and Q-Q plot metrics.

ACIDES limitations: assumes constant selection pressure, negative binomial may not capture sequence-dependent dispersion, cannot describe errors from replicates not sharing initial library, cannot account for mutagenesis between rounds.

edges.reason: Noisy data leads to need for precise error estimation to avoid winner's curse.
Error estimation is needed to decide when to stop experiments.
Problem of stopping criteria motivates design of ACIDES.
ACIDES design includes selectivity estimation with confidence intervals.
Negative binomial model validated against Poisson on plasmid library.
After selectivity estimation, ACIDES corrects ranks and computes RR.
RR and rank correction validated on synthetic data.
Synthetic validation leads to analysis of real datasets.
Low RR in some datasets indicates limitation.
RR analysis enables monitoring of convergence with rounds and depth.
Monitoring leads to comparison with existing methods.
Comparison with Enrich2 and DiMSum validates ACIDES performance.
After validation, limitations of ACIDES are discussed.

must_cover_points: Need for error estimation and winner's curse
Selectivity estimation with negative binomial and softmax
Real dataset analysis with Accuracy and Spread
RR monitoring with rounds and NGS depth
